stem: A pressure vessel shell had an original thickness of 0.5 in. A thickness reading 8 years later is 0.38 in. What is the long-term corrosion rate?
explanation: Long-term corrosion rate = (t_initial − t_actual) / time = (0.5 − 0.38) / 8 = 0.015 in/yr.

stem: A vessel component measures t_actual = 0.360 in., with t_required = 0.150 in. and a corrosion rate of 0.012 in/yr. What is the remaining life?
explanation: Remaining life = (t_actual - t_required) / corrosion rate = (0.360 - 0.150) / 0.012 = 17.5 years.

stem: A component has an actual thickness of 0.445 in. and a required thickness of 0.3 in. What is the remaining corrosion allowance?
explanation: Remaining corrosion allowance = t_actual − t_required = 0.445 − 0.3 = 0.145 in.

stem: The remaining life of a vessel is calculated to be 12 years. Per API 510, the internal/on-stream inspection interval shall not exceed which value (assuming this governs and no RBI assessment is used)?
explanation: API 510 limits the internal or on-stream inspection interval to the lesser of one-half the remaining life or 10 years. One-half of 12 = 6 years, which is less than 10, so 6 years governs.

stem: A shell measures 0.5 in. with a corrosion rate of 0.01 in/yr. For an MAWP assessment covering the next 10-year interval, what thickness should be used (deduct the corrosion expected before the next inspection)?
explanation: t_used = t_measured − (corrosion rate × interval) = 0.5 − (0.01 × 10) = 0.4 in.

stem: A vessel part is located 35 ft below the liquid surface. Using the exam convention (specific gravity = 1.0), what is the static head pressure acting on that part?
explanation: Static head = 0.433 psi/ft × height × SG = 0.433 × 35 × 1.0 = 15.15 psi.

stem: Determine the required thickness of a cylindrical shell for internal pressure (circumferential stress). P = 300 psi, inside radius R = 30 in., S = 17500 psi, E = 1.0. Use t = PR/(SE − 0.6P).
Determine the required thickness of a hemispherical head: P = 400 psi, inside radius R = 30 in., S = 17500 psi, E = 1. Use t = PR/(2SE - 0.2P).
explanation: t = PR/(2SE - 0.2P) = (400 x 30) / ((2 x 17500 x 1) - 0.2 x 400) = 0.3436 in. Using the cylinder formula (0.6953 in) is the common error; a hemi-head needs roughly half the thickness of a cylinder of equal radius.

stem: A Type 1 (double-welded butt) longitudinal joint is fully radiographed. What joint efficiency E is used per Table UW-12?
explanation: For a Type 1 joint with full radiography, Table UW-12 gives E = 1.00. Spot RT would give 0.85 and no RT 0.70.

stem: A vessel has an MAWP of 200 psi. Assuming the test-temperature and design-temperature allowable stresses are equal, what is the standard hydrostatic test pressure per current ASME VIII-1 (UG-99(b))?
explanation: Standard hydrostatic test pressure = 1.3 × MAWP × (S_test/S_design). With the stress ratio = 1, = 1.3 × 200 = 260 psi. (The 1.5× factor is the older pre-1999 basis.)

stem: A fillet weld has a required throat dimension of 0.35 in. Using the exam conversion factor of 0.7, what is the corresponding leg dimension?
explanation: Throat = 0.7 × leg, so leg = throat / 0.7 = 0.35 / 0.7 = 0.5 in.

stem: Per API 510, who has overall responsibility for the in-service inspection program, including compliance with the code?
explanation: API 510 assigns overall responsibility for the inspection, repair, alteration, and rerating program to the owner/user.

stem: API 510 applies primarily to which equipment?
options: In-service vessels: inspection, rating, repair, alteration
New pressure vessels during original code construction
Atmospheric and low-pressure bulk storage tank shells
Buried and aboveground process piping circuit systems
explanation: API 510 covers in-service pressure vessels — inspection, rating, repair, and alteration. New construction is covered by the construction code (e.g., ASME VIII).

stem: When establishing the MAWP for an existing pressure vessel, which thickness should be used in the calculation?
options: The original nominal thickness from the U-1 data report
Actual thickness less corrosion before the next inspection
The minimum thickness value stamped on the nameplate
The as-fabricated thickness at the heaviest shell course
explanation: Rerating/MAWP determinations use the actual measured thickness minus the corrosion allowance that will be lost before the next inspection date.

stem: A vessel may be rerated to a different MAWP only when which condition is met?
options: Informal verbal approval is obtained from the owner/user
The vessel has been in service for more than ten years
Calculations to the construction code, fully documented
A pressure test is substituted for the rating calculation
explanation: Rerating requires calculations per the applicable construction code, documentation, and (typically) updating the nameplate; it is performed by or with the manufacturer/engineer per API 510.

stem: Condition monitoring locations (CMLs) are best described as:
options: Locations where pressure-relief devices are installed
Zones reserved exclusively for welded repair activity
Points established only for recording nameplate data
Designated areas monitored for damage and thickness
explanation: CMLs are designated areas on a vessel where inspections (often thickness readings) are taken to monitor the presence and rate of damage.

stem: An external inspection of an in-service vessel can typically be performed:
options: Only when shut down, opened, and entered
Only during the hydrostatic pressure test
Only by a representative of the jurisdiction
explanation: External inspections are generally on-stream activities; internal inspections require the vessel to be shut down and opened.

stem: The external inspection interval for most pressure vessels is not to exceed:
options: Not to exceed one year under any circumstance
Fixed at ten years regardless of the service
Not to exceed five years (or next internal)
Set at twenty years for all external surfaces
explanation: API 510 generally limits external inspection intervals to 5 years (or the next internal/on-stream inspection, if sooner).

stem: When a risk-based inspection (RBI) assessment is used to set inspection intervals, API 510 requires that it be:
options: Performed only once and never revisited later
Limited strictly to external visual inspection
Reviewed and approved by the welder of record
Documented and updated at the set interval
explanation: RBI assessments must be documented and reassessed/updated periodically and when conditions change (per API 510 referencing API 580).

stem: Which of the following is, by definition, an ALTERATION rather than a repair?
options: Replacing a section with identical material
A change beyond the original design basis
Touch-up of an external protective coating
explanation: An alteration changes the vessel from its original design (e.g., adding a nozzle, increasing MAWP). A repair restores the vessel to a safe operating condition without such a change.
